metric_value: Deployment of Network Stations will lead to reductions in overall desktop computing costs

metric_value: IBM Network Station renamed NetVista 2000; withdrawn April 2002 with no replacement
notes: IBM NetVista thin client line withdrawn April 2002 with no replacement. NC market failed commercially due to PC price commoditization. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_NetVista

metric_value: NC brand never achieved hoped-for popularity; PC prices fell below $1,000 within 2 years, undercutting the NC value proposition. Oracle NC discontinued circa 2000.
notes: post-study, PC commoditization, immature software, and insufficient network bandwidth defeated the NC. Oracle NC discontinued ~2000. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_Computer | Source notes confirm: 'PC commoditization, immature software, and insufficient bandwidth defeated NC. Oracle NC discontinued ~2000.'

metric_name: Java as primary desktop development environment
metric_value: Java will be broadly accepted as primary application development environment for future client/server and network computing
notes: Aberdeen predicts Java will be broadly accepted as primary development environment based on ISV trend

metric_value: Desktop Java failed; server-side Java (JVM/J2EE/Spring) became dominant; JavaScript dominated browser
notes: Java applets/desktop failed; browser JavaScript dominated. Server-side Java became the dominant enterprise platform — partially correct prediction.

metric_value: Google Chromebook (2011+) achieved NC vision: low-cost, browser-centric, centrally managed, sub-$300
notes: Chromebooks vindicated the NC cost/management model 14 years later. Peak education market: 40M+ units. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chromebook

metric_name: VDI/Cloud PC as enterprise NC successor
metric_value: VDI (Citrix, VMware View) and Cloud PC platforms realized the enterprise NC architecture by 2010-2015. The thin-client/centralized model was validated ~15 years after the original NC.
methodology_code: Amazon WorkSpaces) delivered enterprise NC value proposition
notes: historical-record, post-study, The enterprise NC architecture — centralized applications, thin client endpoints — was realized via VDI and Cloud PC platforms 15-20 years later. | VDI/Cloud PC as NC successor confirmed by industry adoption.

metric_value: NC movement explicitly positioned against Wintel dominance; NC Reference Profile designed to reduce Windows dependency
notes: 'Wintel (MS Windows/Intel) PC' named as the alternative the NC was designed to undercut

metric_value: Network Station running Microsoft Office via WinCenter Pro delivers better performance than native PC
notes: IBM claims Network Stations accessing NT-based Microsoft Office via WinCenter Pro deliver better performance than native PC; attributed to more powerful server hardware (128MB RAM vs 16MB PC)

metric_value: Network Station provides upgrade path from legacy 3270/5250 terminals to network computing without disruption
notes: Positioned for customers with legacy block-mode terminals to upgrade to NC without business disruption

metric_name: Operating systems to be obsoleted claim
metric_value: Windows 95, Windows NT, OS/2, Mac OS to be obsoleted by supplier next-generation OS within 18 months
notes: Aberdeen: IS decision makers who understand current OS will be obsoleted within 18 months can commit to NC with minimal risk — this timeframe was aggressive

metric_value: Apple co-announced NC Reference Profile; developing Mac NC (Pippin)
notes: Apple was a co-founder of NC Reference Profile standard; developed Pippin platform as Mac NC

metric_value: Steve Jobs killed Mac NC/Pippin in late 1997; iMac inherited some NC technology
notes: Steve Jobs discontinued all Mac clone efforts in 1997, effectively killing Pippin/Mac NC. Key components inherited by original iMac. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_Computer

metric_value: Java/HTML-based tool for centralized configuration; prevents user desktop modification
notes: IS administrators can configure desktops, establish app authorization, manage display options from central point; users cannot modify desktops

metric_value: Aberdeen encourages IS managers to evaluate IBM Network Station for suitable desktop deployments
notes: Aberdeen encourages IS managers to 'counsel their end-users to evaluate IBM's Network Station' where appropriate

metric_name: NC deployment reduction of IS service costs
metric_value: NC will reduce help desk workload by eliminating user ability to install unauthorized software or tamper with desktop
notes: Help desk benefits: no local disk = users cannot install rogue software or tamper with configuration

metric_name: PC price commoditization defeated NC value proposition
metric_value: PC prices fell below $1,000 quickly (by 1998-1999), destroying the NC cost argument. Wikipedia confirms 'prices of PCs quickly fell below $1,000, making the competition very hard.'
notes: post-study, Wikipedia: 'prices of PCs quickly fell below $1,000, making the competition very hard.' NC cost argument failed. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_Computer | PC price commoditization defeating NC confirmed. Source: Wikipedia NC article.

metric_value: Netscape Navigator 3.0 expected natively implemented in Network Station Q2 1997
notes: IBM working with Netscape to implement Navigator 3.0 natively in Network Station; until then any PC/RS6000 browser supported

metric_value: Netscape acquired by AOL for $4.2B in 1998; Navigator lost browser war to Internet Explorer
notes: Netscape's defeat in the browser wars undermined a key NC technology assumption. IE became dominant; Netscape acquired by AOL 1998.
